text:
This article examines the manifestation of bilingualism within the urban landscape of Cieszyn and Český Těšín, sibling cities divided by the Olza River following the 1920 partition of Cieszyn Silesia. While the region is rooted in a tradition of multi-ethnicity, the modern linguistic environments on the Polish and Czech sides of the border have evolved under distinct legislative and socio-political frameworks. The author explores how Polish and Czech function in public spaces, focusing on the quality and intent of communication in official signage versus informal commercial advertisements.

Through an analysis of authentic linguistic data—including spoken interactions in marketplaces and written texts on storefronts—the study identifies prevalent errors rooted in typological differences between the two Slavic languages. Key areas of investigation include orthographic and graphic discrepancies, the morphological adaptation of loanwords, the pragmatics of politeness and diminutives, and the pitfalls of interlingual homonymy. Furthermore, the article highlights how cultural context shapes linguistic choices, revealing that while bilingualism on the Czech side is often institutional and symbolic of minority rights, it emerges on the Polish side primarily as a pragmatic tool for cross-border commerce. Ultimately, the work illustrates how these linguistic interactions define the mental and material landscape of the borderland.
